text: Laparoscopic splenectomy anatomy is that laparoscopy generally the whenever you perform the massive splenectomy should be avoided and normally unless the spleen is double in the size it will be hidden into the rib cage so it has five ligament that is pancreas splenic ligament gastro splenic ligament phrenicoesplenic ligament, esplenocolic ligament, and phrenicocolic ligament. These all are the five ligaments which is important to keep in mind whenever you do laparoscopic esplenectomy.

Case selection is important. Normal indications are idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, microspherocytosis, benign tumors of the cyst, and HIV-related thrombocytopenia. There are certain contraindications also. Relative contraindication is hematological malignancies and moderate splenomegaly. And the absolute contraindication is massive splenomegaly or if the patient has the portal hypertension, In those situations, it should be avoided.

Patient position. Patient should be semi-lateral position. You will stand in front of the patient. One assistant will be behind the patient. Nurse should be on the right side next to the surgeon to give the instrument. And monitor, either you can one on the left side or you can take two also so that you and your assistant both can see. You have to put one cushion so that the costal margin will be little go up. And this is the usual position of the patient.
The patient is positioned in a semi-lateral orientation with the monitor placed behind them, as part of the setup for the splenectomy procedure.

The surgeon describes the port position for the laparoscopic splenectomy, indicating that it should be placed approximately five to ten centimeters above the umbilicus on the midclavicular line. A small incision is made, and the Veress needle is inserted perpendicularly into the abdominal wall.

After creating pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon performs suction irrigation and the hanging drop test before inserting the trocar. The telescope is introduced, and the arc of visualization is adjusted based on the spleen size. If the spleen is massive, the procedure is contraindicated.
The surgeon illustrates the baseball diamond concept, highlighting the target area of the splenic hilum, which visually resembles the shape of a baseball field or diamond.

The surgeon begins the splenectomy procedure with port placements similar to those used in nephrectomy, noting that the ports are positioned slightly differently, with one port placed lower and another higher.
Because your toilet is shifting down. Yes. Vertically in the same line. And that also depends upon the size of kidney. Exactly same way as here it depends upon the size of the spleen.

Now once you have entered, pneumo-peritoneum also has to be made in the same way. But it should not go vertical otherwise we can damage the bobble. So it should be perpendicular to the abdominal wall. And you will decide the position after putting the telescope. Because it may vary according to the size of the spleen.

The surgeon positions the ports for the splenectomy procedure: the telescope is placed along the mid-capillary line, with two additional ports located at the mid-axillary and posterior axillary lines. An optional epigastric port is used for a liver retractor to elevate the liver.

The surgeon begins the splenectomy by dissecting the inferior ligaments, specifically the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments. Following this, the short gastric vessels are addressed, progressing towards the splenic hilum.

The surgeon uses bipolar, ligature, or harmonic devices to dissect the short gastric vessels, with some opting for endo-GI linear staplers. The surgical field progresses to the hilum, where the vein is separated from the artery, prioritizing the artery first. The kidney is retracted upwards, allowing for the separation of the esplanocolic ligament.
The surgeon uses a hook, tripolar, or harmonic device to carefully dissect near the inferior pole of the spleen, ensuring that the bowel is not injured during the procedure.

The surgeon frees the inferior pole of the spleen, allowing the kidney and spleen to be lifted, enhancing visibility of the short gastric vessels and the hilum. Once the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments are detached, the spleen's lower pole is elevated, facilitating access to the short gastric vessels.

The surgeon applies a titanium clip to control bleeding while dissecting the short gastric vessels. For the hilum, a vascular stapler is recommended for securing the vessels, ensuring a straightforward surgical process.

The surgeon uses a white cartridge stapler through a 12 mm port to secure vessels, applying three rows of staples on either side of the stapler line. The auto suture endo-GIA-II stapler is introduced, and the blade moves after each firing, with an indicator showing tissue thickness. Slight oozing is visible along the stapler line.
The surgeon positions the stapler on the splenic side and vessel side, preparing to fire. Two cartridges may be required for complete vessel transection.

The surgeon uses the auto-suture Endo-GIA-II stapler to fire and separate the remaining short gastric vessels. The frenico-splenic ligament is detached using a hook instrument, completing the separation.
The surgeon introduces the stapler instrument near the spleen, spreading the tissue to facilitate the stapling process without requiring complete skeletonization of the vessels.

The surgeon carefully negotiates the tissue with the stapler, avoiding capturing a complete bunch in one cartridge. The stapler is pressed to lock in place, and the indicator turns green, signaling readiness for firing.

The surgeon uses a powered stapler to fire on the tissue, observing the blade move and cut through the tissue with each firing. The blade is then retracted, indicating the tissue thickness is appropriate for the cartridge.

The surgeon prepares to remove the spleen by using a commercially available D size endo bag with a self-retaining metal ring, ensuring it can accommodate the spleen. The incision may be enlarged to facilitate the removal.

The surgeon uses an auto-switcher endo bag, wrapped inside a reducer, and pushes it into the surgical field. The endo bag, with a metal ring, is deployed in a piston-like manner. The spleen is maneuvered into the endo bag, and a thread is pulled to apply a purse-string suture. An instrument assists in tightening the suture, securing the spleen inside the bag before it is removed from the surgical site.

The surgeon extracts the mouth of the endo bag from the abdominal cavity, while the assistant holds it steady. Using ovum forceps, the surgeon mechanically morselates the spleen into small pieces, placing the tissue into a kidney tray to prevent contamination and avoid rupture of the endo bag. A nasogastric tube is inserted into the splenic fossa as a drain to manage any potential fluid collection.
The surgeon places several stitches to close the wound and applies cyanoacrylate glue for additional closure and sealing.

Injecting little gylocaine if you want you can do. And then the plaster adhesive will be. Just stick there. Now sometime if you have to do the partial splint. In splenic abscess and all. Then you can use the stapler with the gotx tape. In older time, splenectomy was never partial. It was always complete. Total splenectomy was recommended. But now the stapler technology is so advanced that if you want you can do even the partial splenectomy. Even in cases of trauma. Even in cases of trauma it can be used. So we should try to save as much spleen as possible. In this case it was a lobulated abscess.
The surgeon accesses the splenic pedicle and separates it. In case of oozing, a hemostatic agent, flow seal, is applied to control bleeding.
There are many company which is making The backstab is making by the name of flow shield. That is hematinic matrix.
The surgeon is completing the cutting of the spleen, requiring one more cartridge for the stapler to fully separate the tissue.

The surgeon uses a stapler with a Gore-Tex sheath, placing it over the sheath before firing. After removing the red cover from the cartridge, the Gore-Tex sheath is applied. The cut part of the spleen is transferred into an endo bag, followed by manual morselation.
The surgeon performs thorough irrigation and suction in the surgical field, ensuring minimal bleeding. A flow seal can be applied to the area if necessary.

So this small granules of the gelatin and with the human thrombin will make a matrix network and that will keep the complete bleeding separated. And now you can put some irrigation and automatically it will be washed out. Excess of the granules will be separated. And this way you can achieve the hemostasis.

And bleeding can be stopped. Although it is not effective in every type of bleeding, but especially the staple line or the oozers or after myomectomy, it can be very useful and slowly it heals. Once the healing will happen, automatically it comes out. So it has a different type of applicator in the laparoscopy as well as the open surgery.

So you can go to how it works. They have a different clinical information and these type of different applicators. You have the different type of And it has the different type of consistent effective hemostasis. So we will not go in that detail. But you can use it. So the same was used here.

The surgeon uses a Tripolar device, which is a bipolar instrument with a cutting blade, to perform a partial splenectomy. The device allows for coagulation at two points before the blade cuts between them.

The surgeon uses an electrosurgical generator with ligature, bipolar, and monopolar functions to separate the splenic artery and vein. The blade cuts the tissue, ensuring the artery and vein are parallel before firing the vascular stapler.

The surgeon uses a tripolar device, which combines bipolar electrosurgical capabilities with a cutting blade, to perform blunt and electrosurgical dissection of the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments. Sharp dissection is also carried out using the blade.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for dissection, partially separating the splenocolic ligament. Short gastric vessels are carefully dissected, with attention to the tail of the pancreas located below. The procedure aims to stay close to the splenic hilum.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a placelet, following the French technique of lateral mobilization first. The spleen is being mobilized laterally, similar to the approach used in open surgery, before addressing the inferior pole, short gastric vessels, and hilum.
The surgeon mobilizes the spleen laterally, flipping it over the stomach to expose the hilum for further dissection.
Using alone with the energy has the advantage that the cost of a stapler can be saved. But only problem is it is time taking procedure. And you have to be very careful.

The surgeon returns to the inferior pole of the spleen, detaching remaining fibers of the splenocolic ligament after taking several short gastric vessels. A retractor lifts the spleen while a grasper pulls the bowel away, facilitating access to the hilum. The Maryland dissector is used to lift the spleen, ensuring its convexity faces the spleen to prevent injury and bleeding. Suction irrigation is employed to maintain a clear surgical field.

The spleen is now completely free from the colon, revealing the tail of the pancreas. The surgeon approaches the hilum, which is lifted up, and begins to skeletonize the perihilar fat. The vein and artery are visible, and the surgeon checks for an accessory spleen.
The surgeon performs careful dissection around the splenic hilum, creating a visible window between the vein and artery.

The surgeon lifts the inferior pole of the spleen while focusing on the hilum. The artery is identified and held with a Maryland dissector, avoiding the thin vein. The pulsation of the artery is visible as a window is carefully created around it.

A window is created between the vein and artery at the splenic hilum. The surgeon uses energy to seal the artery, intermittently activating the device to shrink the vessel lumen and seal it with denatured protein. The artery is cut after sealing.

The surgeon observes no bleeding as the vein is sealed using a bipolar cutting blade, which discharges energy during activation. The vein is visible, and after a couple of minutes, it collapses as the collected blood returns. The blade is then used to cut the vein, securing the major vessels, making the remaining part of the surgery relatively straightforward.

The surgeon uses a tripolar device to tackle the remaining short gastric vessels near the stomach. The frenico-esplenic ligament is visible and will be separated in the usual fashion. The lateral approach is demonstrated, showing the ease of separating attachments.

The surgeon employs a lateral approach for splenectomy, using a telescope and four ports positioned laterally. Adhesions are cleared with a hook, and the spleen is mobilized laterally without initially addressing the short gastric vessels. A Robbie Dissector is used, and a peanut sponge assists in blunt dissection and controlling bleeding. The phrenico-asplenic ligament is visible, and the spleen is flipped and mobilized similarly to open surgery techniques.

The spleen is completely mobilized and flipped, with its dorsal aspect touching the left lobe of the liver. The stomach is positioned inferiorly. The remaining phrenico-splenic ligaments are visible, and the gastro-hepatic ligament has been separated. The hilum is exposed, showing the artery and vein, with the artery's pulsation observable.

The surgeon approaches the hilum of the spleen, separating perihilar fat and creating a window around the artery and vein. The vascular stapler is used to secure the artery, and the suture is cut simultaneously. The vein is then separated, and another endolinear stapler is fired. The spleen is mobilized, crossing the falciform ligament and touching the gallbladder. The collected blood is suctioned, and the spleen is placed in an endo bag for removal.
